word_list.pronunciation: biː ɔːɹ ɡɛt ɔːɹ stˈeɪ ɪn tˈʌtʃ

senses.definition: to be in contact with someone, particularly by seeing or writing to them regularly

examples.x: I will get in touch with solicitors about this.
Stay in touch with your customers via Internet.
I hope we can stay in touch after you move to another city.

senses.definition: to manage to meet someone in person or contact them on a telephone, through message, etc.

examples.x: I've been trying to get hold of John all day, but his phone seems to be switched off.
If you need to reach me, you can always get hold of me through email or on my cell phone.

senses.definition: to contact a person with a brief message, phone call, or letter

examples.x: When you have time, please drop me a line with your thoughts on the project proposal.
If you need any help or have questions, feel free to drop us a line, and we'll assist you as soon as possible.

senses.definition: used for saying that someone is waiting or talking on a telephone

examples.x: While I was on the line with the customer service representative, I explained my issue and asked for assistance.
The conference call is scheduled for 2 PM, so make sure to be on the line promptly to participate in the meeting.
I've been on the line with my friend for an hour, catching up on all the latest news.
When the important call came in, I was already on the line and ready to discuss the project with the client.
I'll put you on the line with our technical support team, and they can help you troubleshoot the issue you're experiencing.

senses.definition: waiting on the phone until the receiver speaks

examples.x: We try not to keep people on hold for more than a couple of minutes.
After calling the airline to change my flight, I was placed on hold for several minutes while the customer service representative located available options.
When trying to reach the insurance company to inquire about a claim, I spent over 30 minutes on hold before a representative could assist me.
The automated system placed me on hold, and I listened to soothing music while waiting for a customer service agent to address my issue.
The company's call center experienced high call volume, resulting in extended wait times for customers who were placed on hold.

senses.definition: said in a radio communication to indicate that the conversation is over

examples.x: After delivering the status report, the pilot radioed, "Mission accomplished, over and out."
The soldier reported their location to the base camp and then said, "All clear here, over and out."
The police officer radioed for backup and added, "I'll be waiting at the scene, over and out."
The astronaut aboard the spacecraft informed mission control of their successful docking and signed off with "Over and out."
The truck driver communicated with the dispatch center, saying, "I've reached the delivery location, over and out."

senses.definition: to unexpectedly encounter or meet someone or something, often with significant consequences

examples.x: Be cautious when making decisions; you never know when danger might cross your path.
The opportunity of a lifetime may cross your path when you least expect it, so stay vigilant.
It's a small world, and you never know when a past mistake might cross your path and come back to haunt you.

word_list.pronunciation: pˌʊt ˌɛsbˈiː ɪn tˈʌtʃ wɪð ˌɛsbˈiː

senses.definition: to provide someone with the contact information of another person such as their phone number, E-mail, or address

word_list.pronunciation: nˌɑːt sˈiː ɔːɹ fˈaɪnd hˈaɪd nˈɔːɹ hˈɛɹ ʌv ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to have not seen or heard about a person or thing for a considerable amount of time

examples.x: I haven't seen hide nor hair of her since last Friday.
After the storm, we couldn't find hide nor hair of our camping gear.
She promised to visit, but I haven't seen hide nor hair of her all weekend.
The cat is so elusive that sometimes I don't see hide nor hair of her for days.

senses.definition: to be no longer in contact with a friend or acquaintance

examples.x: I used to be close friends with her, but we've gradually lost touch over the years.
After changing jobs, I lost touch with many of my former colleagues.
He moved to a different country, and it's been challenging not to lose touch with his cultural roots.
It's essential to stay in touch with old friends to avoid losing touch with your past.
The rapid pace of technology can make it easy to lose touch with the latest developments in your field if you're not careful.

word_list.pronunciation: kˈiːp hˈoʊld ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ æt ˈɑːɹmz lˈɛŋθ

senses.definition: to not form a friendly relationship with a person or avoid getting involved with something

examples.x: He's a nice guy, but I have to keep him at arm's length until I get to know him better.
The manager held the new project at arm's length until he was sure it was well-planned.
She always keeps her personal life at arm's length from her professional relationships.
I prefer to hold financial investments at arm's length to minimize risk."

word_list.pronunciation: nˌɑːt nˈoʊ ɔːɹ ɹˈɛkəɡnˌaɪz ˌɛsbˈiː fɹʌm ˈædəm

senses.definition: to fail to recognize someone as one has never met them or knows nothing about them

examples.x: She claims to be a distant relative, but I don't know her from Adam.
If you showed me a picture of that actor without context, I wouldn't know him from Adam.
In a big city like this, you pass by countless people daily, and you don't know them from Adam.

senses.definition: to make contact with someone in order to exchange information or to consult with them

examples.x: I'll touch base with you next week to discuss our project's progress.
Let's touch base at the end of the day to ensure we're all on the same page.
I need to touch base with our clients to see if they have any new requirements.
We should touch base with our suppliers to confirm the delivery schedule.
I wanted to touch base and see how you're doing since we last spoke.

word_list.pronunciation: ʃˈɪps ðæt ɔːɹ pˈæs ɪnðə nˈaɪt

senses.definition: a situation where two people or groups cross paths briefly and then continue on their separate ways without any meaningful interaction or connection

examples.x: The two men met once, like ships that pass in the night, and never met again.
We were like ships that pass in the night, meeting once and then going our separate ways.
Our lives intersected briefly, but we were like ships passing in the night, never to meet again.
Sometimes we encounter people who are like ships passing in the night, leaving a lasting impact despite their brief presence.

senses.definition: not having been in contact with someone and unaware of their current situation

examples.x: By the time he was settled in, he became out of touch with that friend.
After moving abroad for several years, I was completely out of touch with my childhood friends.
She had been out of touch with her family for so long that she didn't know about the recent family events.
Being out of touch with the news, I had no idea about the recent political developments.
The professor, having retired for many years, was out of touch with the latest advancements in his field.
